name: Snare Drum
description: The backbone of the kit, providing the signature crack on beats 2 and 4.
tips: Tune batter head to D4 for a full, punchy rock tone.
Resonant head should sit slightly higher than batter for snap.
Tighten lugs in a star pattern (opposite lugs) for even tension.
Tap near each lug — all should ring at the same pitch.
Snare wires need gentle tension; too tight kills sustain.

name: 10" Rack Tom
description: The highest tom, ideal for fast fills and bright, cutting accents.
tips: Should be the highest-pitched tom in the kit.
Tune resonant head a 3rd or 5th above batter for sustain.
Listen for a clear fundamental note with no "wah" wavering.
De-tune one lug very slightly if you hear an oscillating pitch.

name: 12" Rack Tom
description: The middle tom, bridging the gap between the 10" and floor tom.
tips: Pitch should fall between the 10" rack and the floor tom.
Maintain roughly a perfect 5th interval between batter and reso.
A moon gel pad or dampening ring helps control ring.
Use a drum dial to match tension at every lug for consistency.

name: 13" Rack Tom
description: A slightly larger rack tom that sits between the 12" and floor tom in pitch.
tips: Pitch should sit below the 12" and above the floor tom.
Works well in a 10/13 or 12/13 two-rack-tom configuration.
Tune resonant a 3rd or 4th above batter for a warm, full tone.
A moon gel or light dampening tames harsh overtones.
Use a drum dial to match tension at every lug for consistency.

name: 14" Floor Tom
description: The higher floor tom, bridging the rack toms and the deep 16" floor.
tips: Aim for a deep, full tone with good sustain.
Batter head can be tuned slightly lower than resonant.
Tune lower for a fat, boomy sound; higher for more punch.
Check that floor tom legs are tight to prevent sympathetic rattle.
A small strip of moleskin tape can tame harsh overtones.

name: 16" Floor Tom
description: The larger floor tom, delivering the deepest rumble before the bass drum.
tips: Should be noticeably lower in pitch than the 14" floor tom.
A perfect 4th or 5th below the 14" floor sounds musical in fills.
Looser tuning suits rock and jazz; tighter gives more attack.
Check all three legs are even — an uneven drum rings inconsistently.
Light internal dampening (foam strip) controls low-end boom.

name: Bass Drum
description: The foundation of the kit, driving the low-end punch of every groove.
tips: Tighter batter head gives more attack and click.
Looser batter head adds depth and low-end boom.
A small hole in the reso head allows mic placement and tone control.
Pillow or foam muffling on the batter side reduces ring.
Tune both heads evenly, or keep batter slightly higher for punch.
